```typescript
/* ===========================================================================
 * Dead CSS selector characterization (guards the app.css dead-rule cleanup)
 *
 * src/app.css ships several rule blocks whose selectors are never emitted by
 * app.ts, so they have ZERO observable effect on the rendered UI. They are
 * slated for removal. These tests pin the JS-side invariants that make that
 * removal provably safe: the rendered DOM must never produce an element that
 * the to-be-removed selectors could match, and the behaviors those rules
 * appear to provide must be supplied by an independent mechanism that survives
 * the deletion.
 *
 * Removed selectors under test:
 *   (1) `.results-table .folder-row td` — no table row is ever given the
 *       `folder-row` class.
 *   (2) `.breadcrumb .separator`        — breadcrumb separators are class-less
 *       `<span>`s containing the literal "/"; no element carries `separator`.
 *   (3) `.toolbar label.upload-btn` (+ :hover + descendant input) — the upload
 *       label uses `btn`, never `upload-btn`; the wrapped file input is hidden
 *       via the `hidden` HTML attribute, not via the dead `display:none` rule.
 *
 * These assertions pass against the current (pre-cleanup) code AND must keep
 * passing after the dead rules are deleted, because they only depend on the
 * DOM app.ts emits — which the cleanup does not touch.
 * ========================================================================= */
describe('dead CSS selector characterization', () => {
  describe('folder rows never carry the dead `.folder-row` class', () => {
    it('browse table rows (folders, files, and the ".." parent) have no `folder-row` class', async () => {
      const dir = fileEntry({ name: 'sub', path: 'docs/sub', isDirectory: true });
      const file = fileEntry({ name: 'a.txt', path: 'docs/a.txt', isDirectory: false });
      const { results } = await setupCleared();
      renderBrowse(
        browseResult({ path: 'docs/sub', parent: 'docs', entries: [dir, file] }),
        browseRoute('docs/sub'),
      );

      const rows = dataRows(results.querySelector('table')!);
      expect(rows.length).toBeGreaterThanOrEqual(3);
      // No row in the table — folder, file, or parent — carries the class.
      for (const row of rows) {
        expect(row.classList.contains('folder-row')).toBe(false);
        expect(row.className).toBe('');
      }
      // And nowhere in the whole document is there a `.folder-row` element.
      expect(document.querySelectorAll('.folder-row')).toHaveLength(0);
    });

    it('search table rows also never carry the `folder-row` class', async () => {
      const dir = fileEntry({ name: 'd', path: 'docs/d', isDirectory: true });
      const file = fileEntry({ name: 'f.txt', path: 'docs/f.txt', isDirectory: false });
      const { results } = await setupCleared();
      renderSearch({ query: 'q', path: '', results: [dir, file] }, searchRoute('q'));

      const rows = dataRows(results.querySelector('table')!);
      expect(rows).toHaveLength(2);
      for (const row of rows) {
        expect(row.classList.contains('folder-row')).toBe(false);
      }
      expect(document.querySelectorAll('.folder-row')).toHaveLength(0);
    });
  });

  describe('breadcrumb separators are class-less spans (no `.separator`)', () => {
    it('renders one "/" separator per path segment, each as a class-less <span>', async () => {
      const { breadcrumb } = await setupCleared();
      renderBrowse(browseResult({ path: 'a/b/c', entries: [] }), browseRoute('a/b/c'));

      const separators = Array.from(breadcrumb.querySelectorAll('span')).filter(
        (s) => (s.textContent ?? '').trim() === '/',
      );
      // a/b/c → three segments → three separators (Home / a / b / c).
      expect(separators).toHaveLength(3);
      for (const sep of separators) {
        expect(sep.className).toBe('');
        expect(sep.classList.contains('separator')).toBe(false);
      }
      // No element anywhere carries the dead `separator` class.
      expect(document.querySelectorAll('.separator')).toHaveLength(0);
    });

    it('renders no separators (and no .separator) for the root path', async () => {
      const { breadcrumb } = await setupCleared();
      renderBrowse(browseResult({ path: '', entries: [] }), browseRoute(''));

      const slashes = Array.from(breadcrumb.querySelectorAll('span')).filter(
        (s) => (s.textContent ?? '').trim() === '/',
      );
      expect(slashes).toHaveLength(0);
      expect(document.querySelectorAll('.separator')).toHaveLength(0);
    });

    it('search-scope breadcrumbs also use class-less "/" separators', async () => {
      const { breadcrumb } = await setupCleared();
      renderSearch({ query: 'q', path: 'x/y', results: [] }, searchRoute('q', 'x/y'));

      const separators = Array.from(breadcrumb.querySelectorAll('span')).filter(
        (s) => (s.textContent ?? '').trim() === '/',
      );
      expect(separators).toHaveLength(2);
      for (const sep of separators) {
        expect(sep.className).toBe('');
      }
      expect(document.querySelectorAll('.separator')).toHaveLength(0);
    });
  });

  describe('upload control never carries the dead `upload-btn` class', () => {
    it('the upload label uses `btn` (styled by .btn), not `upload-btn`', () => {
      const { uploadLabel } = setup();
      expect(uploadLabel.className).toBe('btn');
      expect(uploadLabel.classList.contains('upload-btn')).toBe(false);
      // The dead selector `.toolbar label.upload-btn` matches nothing.
      expect(document.querySelectorAll('.upload-btn')).toHaveLength(0);
    });

    it('the wrapped file input is hidden via the `hidden` HTML attribute (survives removing the dead `display:none` rule)', () => {
      const { uploadInput, uploadLabel } = setup();
      // The hiding mechanism is the HTML `hidden` attribute, NOT a CSS class on
      // the input or an `upload-btn` ancestor. Removing the dead descendant
      // rule `.toolbar label.upload-btn input[type=file]{display:none}` cannot
      // un-hide it because `hidden` is what does the work.
      expect(uploadInput.hidden).toBe(true);
      expect(uploadInput.hasAttribute('hidden')).toBe(true);
      // The input has no class of its own and no `upload-btn` ancestor, so the
      // dead descendant selector never matched it in the first place.
      expect(uploadInput.className).toBe('');
      expect(uploadLabel.classList.contains('upload-btn')).toBe(false);
    });

    it('the upload control is still rendered inside the toolbar (label + input)', () => {
      const { uploadLabel, uploadInput } = setup();
      const toolbar = document.querySelector('.toolbar')!;
      expect(toolbar.contains(uploadLabel)).toBe(true);
      expect(uploadLabel.contains(uploadInput)).toBe(true);
      expect(uploadInput.type).toBe('file');
    });
  });
});
```

One more consideration: the test for "the wrapped file input is hidden via the hidden HTML attribute (survives removing the dead display:none rule)" — I should be careful about the claim. The `hidden` HTML attribute, per the HTML spec, maps to `display: none` via the UA stylesheet (with a caveat that it can be overridden by author `display` — which is why the app.css.test.ts is careful about dialog display). But for a plain `<input>`, the UA `hidden` → `display:none` works as long as no author rule sets `display` on it. Since the input has no class and app.css has no `input[type=file]` rule except the dead `upload-btn`-descendant one (being removed), the `hidden` attribute does the hiding. So my characterization is correct: after removing the dead rule, `hidden` still hides it. 

Let me verify there's no OTHER rule that could set display on the input. Looking at app.css... the only `input[type=file]` rule is `.toolbar label.upload-btn input[type="file"] { display: none; }`. There's no generic input display rule. And `.toolbar input[type="search"], .toolbar input[type="text"]` rules don't apply to file inputs. So yes, `hidden` is the mechanism. ✓

4. `hidden` attribute test: If app.ts removed `uploadInput.hidden = true` → `uploadInput.hidden` is false, fails. ✓ Sensitive. This is THE key safety: if the JS relied on CSS display:none instead of the hidden attribute, this test would catch that the hiding mechanism is wrong before the CSS is removed.

All tests are genuinely sensitive and not tautological.

Now, the final full run to confirm everything is green.

content.text:
All 264 tests pass (7 new). My work is complete — I only modified the test file `src/app.test.ts`; no production/source code was touched.

## Summary

I wrote **characterization tests** that pin down the JS-side invariants which make the dead-CSS removal provably safe. Since `happy-dom` does not load `app.css` (so `getComputedStyle` can't observe author rules), the strongest available characterization is to prove the **rendered DOM never produces an element the to-be-removed selectors could match**, and that the behaviors those dead rules *appear* to provide are supplied by an independent mechanism that survives the deletion.

I added a new `describe('dead CSS selector characterization')` block (7 tests) to `src/app.test.ts`, covering all three removed selectors plus the file-input hiding mechanism:

1. **`.folder-row`** (dead rule #1) — browse rows (folder, file, `".."` parent) and search rows are verified to have an empty `className` and never carry `folder-row`; `document.querySelectorAll('.folder-row')` must be empty.
2. **`.separator`** (dead rule #2) — breadcrumb separators are verified to be class-less `<span>`s whose text is exactly `/`, with the per-segment count asserted (3 for `a/b/c`, 0 for root, 2 for a search scope `x/y`); `document.querySelectorAll('.separator')` must be empty.
3. **`.upload-btn`** + descendant `input[type=file]` (dead rule #3) — the upload label is verified to use `btn` (never `upload-btn`); the wrapped file input is verified to be hidden via the **`hidden` HTML attribute** (set in JS as `uploadInput.hidden = true`), not the dead `display:none` descendant rule, and carries no class itself — so removing that rule cannot un-hide it.

These pass against the **current** code (verified: 264/264) and depend only on the DOM `app.ts` emits, so they will stay green after the four dead rule blocks are deleted — making the refactor provably behavior-preserving. Each test is genuinely sensitive: any regression that made the JS emit `folder-row`/`separator`/`upload-btn` or drop the `hidden` attribute would make the corresponding `.toHaveLength(0)`/property assertion fail.

I chose not to add CSS-string-parsing assertions to `app.css.test.ts`, because for a pure deletion both "the dead rule exists" and "the dead rule is absent" point the wrong way for a characterization test (one fails before the refactor, one after).

  },

The file input is hidden via uploadInput.hidden = true in app.ts, so removing the dead display:none descendant rule is safe.",
    "The existing test 'renders an upload control: label.btn wrapping a hidden, multiple file input' partially overlaps my upload tests (label class 'btn', hidden attribute) but has distinct structural assertions; my tests add the explicit dead-selector-match checks (.upload-btn/.folder-row/.separator must match nothing) and the input-has-no-class invariant that the dead descendant selector relied on.",
    "Did not add CSS-string assertions to app.css.test.ts: for a pure deletion, asserting a dead rule's presence fails after refactor and asserting its absence fails before — wrong direction for characterization. DOM behavior is the observable invariant preserved."
